CLARITY.justification: The paper is broadly well-organized (Intro → dataset → system → experiments → conclusion) and states key ideas early (ResCeption encoder, RNN for multi-label attributes, inverted index). However, notation and some equations are inconsistently presented (e.g., Eq. (3) mixes indices L and l and the summation ordering is confusing), and there are many formatting/markup problems: duplicated/garbled paragraphs and citation lists at the end, stray footnote markers (e.g. “3”), and broken LaTeX artifacts. Figures and tables are frequently referenced (Fig. 1, Table 1, Fig. 8, etc.) but not shown in the provided text, and several sentences are awkwardly worded (e.g. the description of using textual meta-information), which together make some sections hard to follow.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper mainly combines established components (Inception/Faster R-CNN/GoogleNet, binary hashing, inverted index) and applies them in an e‑commerce fashion-search pipeline; the proposed ResCeption (Sec. 3.1) is essentially adding residual shortcuts to inception modules (closely related to Inception‑ResNet/Szegedy et al.), and the guided ROI/inverted‑index engineering is application‑level. Treating multi‑label prediction as an RNN sequence (Sec. 3.2–3.3) is a known idea (classifier chains/sequence models) and the authors themselves cite similar work (Wang et al. 2016), so the technical novelty is limited. Overall this is an incremental, engineering‑focused integration rather than a highly original methodological advance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper makes plausible, partially supported technical claims (ResCeption improves Inception-v3 convergence with reported ILSVRC numbers; RNN/LSTM is used to model multi-label attribute dependencies; retrieval features evaluated on Holidays/UKB). However, several methodological gaps weaken support: key quantitative results for attribute recognition and guided-ROI mAP are referenced but not shown; there are no comparisons to alternative multi-label baselines (binary relevance, classifier chains), no ablation studies (ordering effects, guided vs unguided) and no statistical analysis. The mathematical exposition is informal but not incorrect in spirit (residual gradient argument and sequence factorization), though some index notation is sloppy. Overall the contributions are plausible but important experimental controls and comparative evidence are missing.
